subject: Biomechanics & Kinesiology
definition: Kinematics: The branch of mechanics that describes the motion of a body or segment without regard to the forces or torques that cause or result from the motion.
Osteokinematics: The study of the gross movement of bones in space relative to the three cardinal planes of the body (sagittal, frontal, transverse) during joint movement.
Arthrokinematics: The study of the microscopic movements that occur between the articular surfaces of a joint during osteokinematic motions (roll, slide/glide, and spin).

Planes of Motion: Intersecting spatial dimensions in which osteokinematic movement occurs: Sagittal (divides body into left/right), Frontal/Coronal (divides body into front/back), and Transverse/Horizontal (divides body into upper/lower).

Axes of Motion: Imaginary pivots around which a bone rotates during movement: Mediolateral axis (perpendicular to Sagittal plane), Anteroposterior axis (perpendicular to Frontal plane), and Vertical/Longitudinal axis (perpendicular to Transverse plane).
Degrees of Freedom: The number of independent planes of angular motion permitted at a given joint, ranging from one (uniaxial, e.g., humeroulnar joint) to three (triaxial, e.g., glenohumeral joint).
Roll: An arthrokinematic motion in which multiple points along one rotating articular surface contact multiple points along another stationary articular surface, like a tire rolling on a road.
Glide (Slide): An arthrokinematic motion in which a single point on one sliding articular surface contacts multiple points on another articular surface, like a locked tire skidding on ice.
Spin: An arthrokinematic motion in which a single point on one rotating articular surface contacts a single point on another articular surface, rotating around a longitudinal axis.

Concave-Convex Rule: A fundamental rule of arthrokinematics stating that when a convex joint surface moves on a stable concave surface, roll and slide occur in opposite directions; conversely, when a concave surface moves on a stable convex surface, roll and slide occur in the same direction.
Open Kinematic Chain (OKC): A movement configuration where the distal segment of an extremity is free to move in space, allowing isolated joint movements and non-predictable adjacent joint patterns.

Closed Kinematic Chain (CKC): A movement configuration where the distal segment of an extremity is fixed or stabilized against a surface, forcing predictable, synchronized motion across all joint segments in the chain.
Goniometry: The clinical technique of measuring the angles of human joint ranges of motion using a specialized instrument (goniometer) aligned with anatomical bony landmarks.

End Feel: The quality of resistance felt by the clinician at the extreme end of passive range of motion, categorized as normal (soft, firm, hard) or abnormal/pathological (empty, boggy, springy block, or early/late muscle spasm).

Joint Play: The small, involuntary accessory movements (such as gliding, rolling, and spinning) that occur between joint surfaces during normal osteokinematic motion. It is not under voluntary control but is essential for pain-free joint function.

Close-Packed Position: The joint position where the articular surfaces are maximally congruent, the joint capsule and major ligaments are maximally taut, and the joint is locked and mechanically most stable.

Loose-Packed (Resting) Position: Any joint position other than the close-packed position; specifically, the position of maximum resting where the joint capsule and ligaments are most slack, allowing maximum joint play and greatest joint cavity volume.

Capsular Pattern: A specific, predictable pattern of proportionate limitation in joint range of motion (described by Cyriax) indicating that the entire joint capsule is involved, such as in arthritis or capsulitis.
principle: Cardinal Planes and Axes Relationship: Osteokinematic rotation always occurs within a plane of motion and around an axis that is perpendicularly oriented to that plane (e.g., flexion/extension occurs in the sagittal plane around a mediolateral axis).

Application of the Concave-Convex Rule: Understanding the direction of glide relative to roll is essential for manually restoring joint range of motion. For example, to restore shoulder abduction (convex humeral head moving on concave glenoid fossa), manual glide must be applied in the inferior direction (opposite of the superior osteokinematic roll).

Joint Constraint and Degrees of Freedom: The anatomical geometry of the joint surfaces and the arrangement of surrounding collateral ligaments dictate the joint's degrees of freedom and protect it from non-physiological translations.

Kinematic Chain Load Distribution: Closed kinematic chain movements distribute weight-bearing loads across multiple joint systems, evoking co-contraction of muscle groups and reducing shear stresses on individual ligaments.

End Feel Interpretation: Clinicians assess end feel to evaluate the physiological structures restricting joint movement. A hard end feel represents bone-to-bone contact, a firm end feel represents capsular or ligamentous tension, and a soft end feel represents soft tissue approximation.

Clinical Significance of Joint Play: Normal joint play is a prerequisite for full, pain-free osteokinematic movement. Loss of joint play (accessory motion) results in restricted active and passive range of motion, and attempting to force osteokinematic motion without restoring joint play causes pain and cartilage damage.

Application of Loose-Packed Position: Because the loose-packed position allows for maximum joint play and minimal capsular tension, it is the optimal position for clinical assessment of joint mobility and the application of manual joint mobilization techniques.

Characteristics of Close-Packed Position: In this mechanically stable position, joint cavity volume is minimized, and the joint cannot be easily distracted. It is the position most susceptible to injury from external forces due to the lack of 'give' in the taut capsuloligamentous structures.

Capsular Pattern Assessment (Cyriax Concept): Identifying a capsular pattern helps clinicians differentiate between total joint pathology (like osteoarthritis) and non-capsular lesions (like muscle strains). For example, the classic capsular pattern of the shoulder exhibits the greatest restriction in external rotation, followed by abduction, and then internal rotation.
equipment: Universal Goniometer (360-degree and 180-degree variations) for measuring joint angles.
Inclinometers and Digital Goniometers for spine and extremity range of motion analysis.
Gravity-dependent goniometers (bubble inclinometers) for precise neck and trunk measurements.

parameters.goniometerAlignment: Stationary arm parallel to proximal bone axis, moving arm parallel to distal bone axis, fulcrum over joint axis.
parameters.goniometerError: Acceptable clinical goniometry measurement error is typically +/- 5 degrees.
parameters.guniometerStandard: Neutral-Zero Method (starting all joint range of motion measurements at 0 degrees).

clinicalPearls: Applying the concave-convex rule is the foundation of joint mobilization. When treating shoulder stiffness with inferior glides, you are physically facilitating inferior arthrokinematic sliding to allow the superior roll required for pain-free shoulder abduction.

An abnormal 'empty' end feel occurs when the patient restricts movement due to intense pain before any physical tissue resistance is encountered. This is highly suggestive of acute pathology like joint infection, acute bursitis, or underlying neoplasm.

In goniometric alignment, the fulcrum does not remain perfectly stationary over the joint axis throughout the entire range of motion because human joints are not perfect hinges; they have instant centers of rotation that shift dynamically.

Closed kinematic chain exercises (like squats) are highly functional because they mimic weight-bearing daily living activities, stimulate joint mechanoreceptors, and evoke coordinated co-contraction of the hamstring and quadriceps groups.

Normal firm end feels are produced by tension in joint capsules, ligaments, or muscles (e.g., knee extension or ankle dorsiflexion). An early firm end feel suggests joint contracture or adaptive shortening of these tissues.
vivaQuestions: Question: State the Concave-Convex Rule and explain its clinical significance in manual joint mobilization. | Answer: The rule states that if a convex surface moves on a stable concave surface, roll and slide occur in opposite directions; if a concave surface moves on a stable convex surface, roll and slide occur in the same direction. It is clinically significant because manual glides must be applied in the direction of the slide (not the roll) to properly restore limited joint range of motion. | Examiner Key Points: Convex-on-concave vs concave-on-convex, direction of roll and slide, therapeutic application. | Clinical Importance: Guides the correct application direction of manual therapy joint mobilization techniques to minimize articular shear.

Question: Differentiate between normal and pathological end feels, giving a clear clinical example of each. | Answer: A normal end feel is the physiological restriction of a joint's motion (e.g., soft tissue approximation in elbow flexion, capsular firm in knee extension, bone-to-bone hard in elbow extension). A pathological end feel is abnormal (e.g., empty end feel due to severe pain, or a springy block indicating a torn meniscus blocking movement). | Examiner Key Points: Normal vs abnormal types, physical tissue limitations, clinical conditions. | Clinical Importance: Helps the clinician identify the restricting pathology, helping differentiate between articular, extra-articular, inflammatory, or mechanical blocks.

Question: Explain why human joints have changing 'instant centers of rotation' instead of static axes of rotation. | Answer: Human joint surfaces are not perfectly symmetrical spheres or cylinders, and their movement consists of combined rolling, sliding, and spinning rather than simple hinge rotation. Therefore, the axis of rotation shifts dynamically throughout the range of motion. | Examiner Key Points: Non-congruent surfaces, combination of roll/glide, instant center of rotation (ICR). | Clinical Importance: Explains why goniometric alignment requires aligning the moving arm with the long axis of the moving bone rather than focusing entirely on a fixed fulcrum.

Question: Define 'Degrees of Freedom' and list the degrees of freedom for the elbow joint vs. the hip joint. | Answer: Degrees of freedom refer to the number of independent planes of angular movement permitted at a joint. The humeroulnar (elbow) joint is a uniaxial hinge joint with 1 degree of freedom (flexion/extension in the sagittal plane). The hip joint is a triaxial ball-and-socket joint with 3 degrees of freedom (flexion/extension, abduction/adduction, and internal/external rotation). | Examiner Key Points: Plane definition, uniaxial vs triaxial, joint structural examples. | Clinical Importance: Guides rehabilitation progression, joint stabilization strategies, and assessment of complex multiaxial joint pathologies.

Question: How do Open Kinematic Chain (OKC) and Closed Kinematic Chain (CKC) exercises differ in terms of muscle activation and joint loading? | Answer: In OKC, the distal segment is free, allowing isolated single-joint movement and high shear forces. In CKC, the distal segment is fixed to a surface, creating multi-joint, coordinated movement that increases joint compression and stimulates co-contraction of agonist and antagonist muscles. | Examiner Key Points: Distal segment status, isolated vs coordinated movement, shear vs compressive loading. | Clinical Importance: Essential for structuring post-operative protocols (e.g., ACL reconstruction) where shear forces must be avoided in early healing phases.

Question: Describe the planes and axes of motion for hip abduction and adduction. | Answer: Hip abduction and adduction occur within the Frontal (Coronal) plane around an Anteroposterior (AP) axis that passes horizontally from front to back through the center of the femoral head. | Examiner Key Points: Frontal plane, AP axis, perpendicular orientation of plane and axis. | Clinical Importance: Crucial for accurate positioning of a patient and goniometer during pelvic and hip clinical assessments.

Question: What is an 'empty' end feel, and what does it indicate to a physiotherapist? | Answer: An empty end feel is a pathological restriction where the clinician cannot reach physical end-range resistance because the patient stops the movement due to intolerable pain. It indicates acute inflammation, joint infection, active bursitis, fracture, or neoplasm. | Examiner Key Points: Absence of physical barrier, pain-limited stop, serious underlying pathology. | Clinical Importance: Serves as a red flag, indicating that vigorous stretching or joint mobilization is strictly contraindicated.

Question: Explain the difference between osteokinematics and arthrokinematics. | Answer: Osteokinematics deals with the gross, visible movements of bone shafts in space (such as flexion or abduction). Arthrokinematics deals with the small, involuntary movements that occur between the opposing joint surfaces (such as rolling, gliding, and spinning). | Examiner Key Points: Bone movement vs joint surface movement, voluntary control vs accessory motion. | Clinical Importance: A joint must have normal arthrokinematics (accessory glide) to achieve pain-free, full osteokinematics (active range of motion).

Question: What are the three primary variables that influence joint Range of Motion (ROM)? | Answer: Joint range of motion is primarily influenced by: 1. Joint surface geometry and congruency. 2. Extensibility of periarticular connective tissues (capsule, ligaments, tendons). 3. Muscle bulk and flexibility of overlying musculotendinous units. | Examiner Key Points: Articular shape, ligamentous/capsular extensibility, muscle tension/bulk. | Clinical Importance: Directs the therapist's diagnostic tests to identify whether a movement restriction is capsular, muscular, or bony in origin.

Question: What is the neutral-zero method of goniometric measurement, and why is it standard? | Answer: The neutral-zero method defines the starting position for all joint range of motion measurements as 0 degrees, which corresponds to the anatomical position of the joint. It is standard to ensure consistent, reproducible communication of joint limitations among healthcare professionals. | Examiner Key Points: Anatomical starting position, 0-degree reference, standardization. | Clinical Importance: Prevents misinterpretation of clinical notes and ensures accurate tracking of patient recovery progress over time.

mcqs.question: Which plane divides the human body into left and right halves?
mcqs.explanation: The sagittal plane runs anteroposteriorly, dividing the body into left and right halves. Flexion and extension occur within this plane.

mcqs.question: Rotation in the transverse plane occurs around which axis of motion?
mcqs.explanation: Movement in the transverse (horizontal) plane occurs around a vertical or longitudinal axis of motion (e.g., spinal rotation or hip internal/external rotation).

mcqs.question: The glenohumeral joint is classified as having how many degrees of freedom?
mcqs.explanation: The glenohumeral joint is a ball-and-socket joint that permits motion in all three cardinal planes (flexion/extension, abduction/adduction, internal/external rotation), giving it three degrees of freedom.

mcqs.question: According to the concave-convex rule, when the convex femoral head moves on the stable concave acetabulum during hip abduction, what is the relation of roll and slide?
options: Roll and slide occur in the same direction
Roll and slide occur in opposite directions
mcqs.answer: Roll and slide occur in opposite directions
mcqs.explanation: Since the moving femoral head is a convex surface moving on a stable concave acetabular cup, the arthrokinematic roll and slide occur in opposite directions (roll occurs superiorly, slide occurs inferiorly).

mcqs.question: Which of the following joint actions is a classic example of a closed kinematic chain (CKC) movement?
options: Seated leg extension on a machine
A biceps curl with a dumbbell
An active leg swing in side-lying
mcqs.explanation: In a squat, the feet are fixed to the ground (distal segment fixed), making it a closed kinematic chain movement that requires synchronized motion at the ankle, knee, and hip joints.

mcqs.question: An abnormal end feel characterized by a bouncy, springy rebound before full range of motion is achieved is highly suggestive of:
options: A displaced intra-articular structure, such as a meniscus tear
mcqs.answer: A displaced intra-articular structure, such as a meniscus tear
mcqs.explanation: A springy block end feel is pathological and is caused by a mechanical block, typically a displaced piece of cartilage or meniscus, rebounding within the joint.

mcqs.question: Which goniometric landmark is typically used as the fulcrum for measuring knee flexion?
mcqs.explanation: To measure knee flexion and extension, the goniometer fulcrum is placed over the lateral epicondyle of the femur, with the arms aligned with the greater trochanter and lateral malleolus.

mcqs.question: If a joint has an 'empty' end feel, the clinician must:
options: Apply vigorous passive stretching to break adhesions
Apply grade IV manual joint oscillations
Stop the movement immediately and refer for medical evaluation
Use local heat to relax the muscular resistance
mcqs.answer: Stop the movement immediately and refer for medical evaluation
mcqs.explanation: An empty end feel is a clinical red flag representing a severe acute inflammatory or structural lesion where pain prevents reaching end-range tissue resistance; further force is contraindicated.

mcqs.question: An arthrokinematic motion where multiple points on one surface contact multiple points on another surface is defined as:
mcqs.explanation: Roll is defined as the contact of multiple points on one articulating surface with multiple points on the opposing surface, similar to a wheel rolling on flat ground.

mcqs.question: Which joint in the human body is classified as a uniaxial hinge joint?
mcqs.explanation: The humeroulnar joint of the elbow is a uniaxial hinge joint that permits only flexion and extension within the sagittal plane around a mediolateral axis.

reference: Norkin, P. K., & Levangie, P. K. (2011). Joint Structure and Function: A Comprehensive Analysis (5th ed.). F.A. Davis Company.
Neumann, D. A. (2016). Kinesiology of the Musculoskeletal System: Foundations for Rehabilitation (3rd ed.). Elsevier.
Houglum, P. A., & Bertoti, D. B. (2012). Brunnstrom's Clinical Kinesiology (6th ed.). F.A. Davis Company.
